---
title: "Reflection"
---

## What I Learned

Throughout this module, I developed a range of technical and analytical 
skills that I had not previously worked with in a structured way. This 
portfolio represents not just the final result, but the process of 
learning, failing, and improving.

## Git and Version Control

Learning to use Git and GitHub was one of the most valuable skills I 
acquired. At first, the concept of commits, branches and push/pull felt 
overwhelming. However, through consistent practice I now understand how 
to track changes, write meaningful commit messages, and manage a project 
repository professionally. I can see how these skills are essential in 
any data or technology-driven workplace.

## Quarto and Web Development

Building this ePortfolio with Quarto taught me how websites are structured 
and how to customise them using YAML, SCSS and Markdown. I gained a deeper 
appreciation for how design choices, typography, colour and layout affect 
the user experience. One of my biggest challenges was configuring the 
deployment pipeline through GitHub Pages, which required careful attention 
to folder structure and settings.

## Python and Data Analytics

Working with Python for the Diabetes Prediction project deepened my 
understanding of machine learning concepts such as Decision Trees, 
Random Forest, recall and model evaluation. I learned that choosing 
the right metric matters, for example in healthcare, minimising false negatives 
is more important than overall accuracy.

## Challenges and Growth

This module pushed me outside my comfort zone. One of the most 
persistent technical challenges I faced was a Windows Defender conflict 
with Quarto's rendering process. Every time Quarto attempted to copy 
image files into the docs folder, Windows Defender blocked the operation 
with a PermissionDenied error. Resolving this required me to investigate 
system-level settings, add folder exclusions to Windows Security, and 
develop a structured debugging approach, skills I had never needed before.

Beyond technical issues, I encountered moments of genuine frustration 
where nothing seemed to work. Working through these challenges 
independently taught me resilience and problem-solving skills that go 
beyond the technical content itself. I learned that debugging is not 
a sign of failure but it is a core part of working with real tools in 
real environments.

## Looking Ahead

These skills are directly applicable to my career goals in finance, 
strategy and data analytics, where data-driven decision making is 
increasingly important. I plan to continue developing my Python skills 
and explore tools such as SQL and Tableau to complement what I have 
learned here.
